flags: ORDER DEFECT in the source: step 6 hangs the finished piece on the wall and step 7 then tells you to brush on three coats of spar urethane. The rewrite swaps those two steps so the finish goes on before the board is hung.

Cup-hook spacing is not authored. The '2 1/2" apart, about 1 3/4" clear at each end' figure is DERIVED arithmetic from the cut list's 16" starting length and the 6 cup hooks in materials, and is offered as guidance since the plan explicitly makes the board length the builder's choice.
The cut list's own note says 16" is only a starting size, so no lumber-yield claim is made beyond the fact that one 6 ft (72") picket covers any single-board length.

ops.title: Size and cut the board
ops.body: Count the leashes, collars and brushes this has to carry and lay them out side by side, then add room for whatever wording you want to paint on. The cut list's starting size is a single hanger board 16" long, and a cedar fence picket is about 5/8" thick and 5 1/2" wide, so that dimension is the only one you actually choose — the width and thickness come with the board. Cut the picket square to your chosen length; a 6 ft picket is 72" long, so one board covers any single-board length you settle on with room to spare. Cut off the picket's dog-eared point if your board includes it, unless you want to keep that shape as the top of the hanger.

ops.title: Sand smooth
ops.body: A fence picket comes rough-sawn, and stain sinks into torn fibers far darker than into sanded wood, so this is what decides whether the color lands evenly. Work the face, both long edges and both ends with the sander, starting coarse enough to knock the mill fuzz off and finishing fine. Ease the four corners and the arrises slightly so nothing catches a hand reaching for a leash. Vacuum or wipe the dust off before any stain touches it.

ops.title: Apply the stain
ops.body: Wipe stain onto the front face, both long edges and both ends, and leave the back bare if the board will sit flat against the wall — an unstained back costs nothing and saves a coat. Work it in with a rag or brush and wipe the excess back off before it gets tacky; cedar is soft and blotches if stain is left to pool. Let it dry fully before you lay any lettering over it, or the paint will lift the stain.

ops.title: Install the hooks and hangers
ops.body: Mark the hook positions along a single line so they hang level, spacing the six cup hooks evenly — on the 16" starting length, six hooks 2 1/2" apart span 12 1/2" and leave about 1 3/4" clear at each end. Predrill every hook location with a bit slightly smaller than the hook's screw shank; cedar is soft enough that an undrilled hook will crush its own threads or split the board. Thread the cup hooks in by hand until they seat, turning them all so the openings face the same way. On the back, nail one sawtooth picture hanger near each end rather than a single center hanger — two spread points is what keeps a loaded board from tipping or pivoting on the wall.

ops.title: Apply the final finish
ops.body: Brush exterior spar urethane over the whole piece — face, edges, ends and around the lettering — before it goes on the wall, so you can reach every surface and lay the board flat while each coat levels. Spar urethane rather than an interior poly is the right call for anything near an entry door, where wet leashes and outside air do the damage. Apply three coats, letting each dry fully; no sanding between coats is needed. If a cup hook is in the way, back it out, finish under it, and thread it back into its existing hole.

ops.title: Hang it up
ops.body: Measure the exact distance between the two sawtooth hangers on the back and transfer that spacing to the wall, marking both points off one level line rather than measuring each down from the ceiling. Drive wall anchors or screws sized for a loaded board — several leashes and collars is more weight than a picture — and hook the board on. Check it with a level and adjust before you load it up.

flags: MATERIAL-ASSIGNMENT DEFECT (bench 1x12): the cut list assigns the two 17 1/4" sides AND the 48" seat to the 6 ft (72") 1x12 — that is 82 1/2" of parts from a 72" board and cannot work. The total yield is fine; the rewrite reassigns the parts (8 ft board: 46 1/2" bottom + both 17 1/4" sides + one 13 1/4" divider = 94 1/4" of cuts; 6 ft board: 48" seat + the second divider = 61 1/4"). Verified with a 1/8" kerf on each board.

MATERIAL-ASSIGNMENT DEFECT (shelf 1x8): the cut list assigns both 46 1/2" shelf boards AND the two 8 1/2" dividers to the 8 ft board — the two 46 1/2" pieces alone use 93 1/8" of 96". The dividers move to the 10 ft board, which then holds 49 1/2" + 2 x 16 1/2" + 2 x 8 1/2" = 100" of 120". No shortfall.

The bench bottom's height inside the sides is not authored. It is DERIVED: 17 1/4" side less the 3/4" bottom less the 13 1/4" divider puts the bottom board 3 1/4" up from the floor ends of the sides, which is roughly where the 3 1/2" footer trim wraps. Confirm before cutting.

run1-box-geometry.mjs paired parts across the two separate units (it matched 'Bench footer sides' against 'Shelf main shelf/back'). The numeric finding it proved — a 46 1/2" member captured between two 3/4" members giving 48" outside — is used, and it holds for BOTH units independently: 46 1/2" + 3/4" + 3/4" = the 48" bench seat and the 48" shelf outside width.

step.title: Cut all the parts
step.body: Cut the bench parts from the 1x12s (actual size 3/4" x 11 1/4"): one bottom at 46 1/2", two dividers at 13 1/4", two sides at 17 1/4", and one seat at 48". Take the bottom, both sides and one divider off the 8 ft board and the 48" seat plus the second divider off the 6 ft board — do NOT follow the cut list's material column here, which puts the seat and both sides on the 6 ft board and asks for 82 1/2" of parts from a 72" board. From the two 8 ft 1x4s cut one top back trim at 48", two top side trims at 12", two footer sides at 11 1/4" and one footer front at 49 1/2" (footer front, both side trims and one footer side on the first board; the back trim and the second footer side on the second). For the shelf, cut both 46 1/2" shelf/back boards from the 8 ft 1x8, and cut the two 16 1/2" sides, the 49 1/2" top front and both 8 1/2" dividers from the 10 ft 1x8 — the dividers will not fit on the 8 ft board alongside the two 46 1/2" pieces. Cut the 49 1/2" shelf top back from the 6 ft 1x2, and cut the two plywood backs from the 48" x 24" quarter-inch panel: 48" x 14" for the bench and 48" x 10" for the shelf, which together use the panel's full 24" width exactly. Square every cut and label each piece.

ops.title: Build the bench base
ops.body: The two 17 1/4" sides run the full height and everything else fits between them: the 46 1/2" bottom plus the two 3/4" sides makes 48" overall, which is exactly the length of the seat that lands on top. Stand the sides up, set the bottom between them, and fasten through the outside faces with 2" screws and glue, or with pocket holes and 1 1/4" screws driven from underneath so nothing shows. Then mark the two divider positions on the bottom board and square them up before fastening — the dividers set the cubby widths for whatever goes underneath, so measure them off one end and check both marks against each other. The 13 1/4" dividers are shorter than the full side height, which puts the bottom board about 3 1/4" up from the floor ends of the sides where the footer trim will wrap; check that before you fasten, because the dividers have to land on the bottom and reach the underside of the seat.
materials: Self-tapping wood screws, 2", or pocket hole screws, 1-1/4"

ops.title: Attach the seat
ops.body: Nail the 48" top back trim along the back edge of the 48" seat with 1 1/4" brads and glue, keeping both ends flush — the two boards are the same length, so a flush end on one end proves the other. Set the seat down on the base with its ends flush to the outside faces of the sides and its back edge over the back of the carcass, then fasten it with 2" screws down into the sides and dividers, or with pocket holes drilled up through the sides so the seat face stays clean. Push down on the middle of the seat as you drive the screws over the dividers; a divider that is a hair short leaves a gap you can pull shut now and never can later.
materials: Self-tapping wood screws, 2", or pocket hole screws, 1-1/4"

ops.title: Add the bench trim
ops.body: The trim is all cut to fit, so hold each piece in place and mark it against the assembled bench rather than trusting the listed length. The 49 1/2" footer front laps the full 48" outside width plus the 3/4" thickness of a footer side at each end, so fit the two 11 1/4" footer sides first and let the front cover their end grain. The two 12" top side trims run under the ends of the seat to match the back trim. Glue and 1 1/4" brads hold all of it; nail into the sides and the bottom board, not into thin air, and fill the nail holes later with the wood filler.

ops.title: Attach the bench back
ops.body: Cut the bench back 48" x 14" from the quarter-inch plywood with a circular saw and keep the offcut — the shelf back at 48" x 10" comes out of the same 24"-wide panel and the two together use its full width, so a wandering cut costs you the second piece. Set the back on with its long edge flush to the outside width and fasten with 3/4" brads into the sides, the bottom and both dividers. A back nailed on square is what locks the whole carcass out of racking, so check a diagonal before the second edge goes down.

ops.title: Build the shelf
ops.body: The shelf is built like the bench: the two 16 1/2" sides run full height and the 46 1/2" boards sit between them, making the same 48" outside width. One 46 1/2" board is the hook board standing on edge at the back and the other is the horizontal shelf that lands on top of it — the side height works out exactly, with the 7 1/4" hook board plus the 3/4" shelf plus the 8 1/2" dividers filling the 16 1/2" side. Cut the top front corner off each side at an angle before assembly rather than leaving a square point at eye level, and cut both sides together or trace one from the other so the angles match. Glue and 1 1/4" brads through the sides into the ends of the shelf boards; then stand the two 8 1/2" dividers on the shelf, marked off one end so the bays come out even.

ops.title: Attach the shelf top
ops.body: The top is two boards: the 49 1/2" x 7 1/4" top front and the 49 1/2" x 1 1/2" top back, which together make a top about 8 3/4" deep over 7 1/4"-deep sides. Both are 49 1/2" against the shelf's 48" outside width, so center them and you get a 3/4" overhang at each end. Fasten with glue and 1 1/4" brads down into the side tops and the divider tops, keeping the back edge of the 1x2 flush with the back of the carcass so the whole unit still sits flat to the wall.

ops.title: Attach the shelf back and hooks
ops.body: Fasten the 48" x 10" plywood back on with glue and 3/4" brads into the sides, the shelf and both dividers, checking a diagonal first — same job as on the bench, squaring the unit permanently. Then lay out the three double hooks across the 46 1/2" hook board, marking them evenly off one end and all on one level line, and predrill before driving their screws so a 3/4" board does not split. Mount the hooks now, while the shelf is on a bench and you can reach both faces, rather than after it is on the wall.

ops.title: Finish and hang
ops.body: Fill the nail and screw holes, let the filler dry, then sand both pieces smooth and ease every edge — especially the front edge of the bench seat and the angled shelf corners. Prime and paint or stain and seal both units before hanging, while you can still lay them flat. To hang the shelf, find a wall stud, hold the unit at the height you want with a level on the top, and drive a long screw through the hook board into the stud; the hook board is the thickest thing on the back and the only part strong enough to carry loaded hooks. Fill and touch up that screw head afterward, and set the bench under it.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

baseStepTitles: Attach the shelf back and hooks

flags: CUT LIST INCOMPLETE: the materials list includes a 2x6 8 ft for the toekick build-up and a 1x2 8 ft, but neither appears in the cut list, so no toekick or 1x2 part has a length. The rewrite tells you to cut them to your own numbers and does not invent one.

MISSING PART: step 5 (and the description) call for a 'center support' tying the front support to the bottom, but there is no center support row in the cut list. The rewrite describes the job it does and says to cut it to fit rather than asserting a length.

UNRESOLVED DIMENSION: the front support is listed at 24 3/4", but the cabinet's clear interior width is 34 1/2" (proved below), so the front support cannot be a full-width front rail. The rewrite does not assert its orientation.

run1-box-geometry.mjs: the 29 1/4" sides run the full height and the 34 1/2" bottom and supports sit BETWEEN them, proved by the 36" x 29 1/4" back panel (34 1/2" + 3/4" + 3/4" = 36"). Overall box is 36" wide x 29 1/4" tall x 23" deep. All prose follows that reading.

ops.title: Cut all the parts
ops.body: Rip the 3/4" plywood to EXACTLY 23" wide and keep the fence untouched for every strip — on a frameless cabinet there is no face frame to hide a rip that wanders, and the error shows as a visible step where two cabinets meet. From those strips cut two sides 23" x 29 1/4" and one bottom 23" x 34 1/2". From the two 8 ft 1x4s cut three supports at 34 1/2" and one front support at 24 3/4" — two 34 1/2" pieces plus the 24 3/4" piece fit on the first board and the third 34 1/2" piece comes off the second, which is why two boards are listed. Cut the 1/4" back panel 36" x 29 1/4". The 2x6 for the toekick build-up and the 1x2 have no lengths in the cut list, so leave them until the box is assembled and cut them to what you measure. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.
materials: Plywood, 3/4", cabinet grade or hardwood, sheet

ops.title: Edge-band the exposed edges
ops.body: Iron edge banding onto every plywood edge that will show at the front of the finished cabinet — the front edge of both sides and the front edge of the bottom — while the pieces are still flat on the bench. Run the iron slowly enough to melt the adhesive through, press the banding down with a block as it cools, then trim it flush and sand the corner just enough to break the arris without cutting through the veneer. Doing this before assembly is the whole point: once the box is together, the inside corners are a losing fight for an iron. Skip it only if the cabinet is getting painted.
materials: Plywood, 3/4", cabinet grade or hardwood, sheet

ops.title: Attach the bottom to the sides
ops.body: The two 29 1/4" sides run the full height of the cabinet and the 34 1/2" bottom fits between them, which makes the finished box 36" wide overall — the same 36" as the back panel, and the standard width for a sink base. Drill 3/4"-setting pocket holes along both ends and the underside edges of the bottom panel, then clamp it between the sides and drive 1 1/4" pocket screws with glue in the joint. Keep the bottom's front edge flush with the sides' front edges. Check both diagonals across the U you have just built before the glue grabs; every part that follows registers off this assembly.
materials: Plywood, 3/4", cabinet grade or hardwood, sheet

ops.title: Attach the top supports
ops.body: Two of the three 34 1/2" 1x4 supports go across the top of the box — one at the front edge and one at the back — fitting between the sides exactly as the bottom does. Drill their pocket holes so the holes face UP: nothing sits above them, so they are hidden by the countertop and you still get the stronger end-grain-free joint. Fasten with 1 1/4" pocket screws and glue, keeping the top edges flush with the tops of the sides so the countertop lands on a flat plane. Recheck the box for square across the top once both are in.

ops.title: Add the back and center supports
ops.body: The third 34 1/2" support is the back support, fitted between the sides at the top rear and tied into the top support as well — this is the part the cabinet hangs from once it is screwed to the wall studs, so it takes glue and pocket screws into both the sides and the adjoining top support. Then fit the front support and a center support that ties it down to the bottom panel; that pair is what stops a sink base's unusually wide, unsupported front opening from racking under a loaded countertop and a full sink. The cut list has no row for the center support and gives the front support as 24 3/4", which is not the cabinet's 34 1/2" clear width, so measure the actual gap in your assembled box and cut both to fit rather than to the list. Fasten everything with 1 1/4" pocket screws and glue.

ops.title: Attach the back panel
ops.body: The 36" x 29 1/4" back panel is the exact outside footprint of the assembled box, so it is also your final squaring tool. Start from two factory edges of the plywood, line one corner up with a corner of the cabinet, and fasten that edge first with glue and 3/4" staples or brads; then pull the opposite corner into line with the panel's edge before nailing it. Nail into the sides, the bottom and the back support all the way around, roughly every 4" to 6". Once this panel is on square, the cabinet cannot rack.

ops.title: Hang the doors
ops.body: Measure your actual opening rather than working from the list, then size the two doors so that together they cover the full 36" outside width of the box with a consistent gap between them — a frameless cabinet has no frame to hide behind, so the doors ARE the front. Use 1/2" overlay concealed euro hinges rated for frameless cabinets; standard face-frame overlay hinges bore and mount to a different geometry and will not sit right on plywood edges. Bore the cup holes in the doors, screw the mounting plates to the inside faces of the sides, hang the doors, and then use the hinges' own adjustment screws to bring the gap and the door faces into line. Adjust both doors against each other, not one at a time, so the reveal down the middle stays even.

baseStepTitles: Attach the bottom to the sides
Add the back and center supports

flags: LUMBER SHORTFALL (verified with packBoards, 1/8" kerf, 96" stock): every 1 1/2" x 3 1/2" part in the cut list — 2 posts at 20", 8 diagonals at 10 3/4", 4 long diagonals at 21 3/4", 4 stretchers at 21", 3 cross stretchers at 59" and 2 cross braces at 30 3/4" — totals 535 1/2" and needs SIX 8 ft 2x4s (proved lower bound 6). The materials list supplies five. Buy a sixth.

ZERO TRIM ALLOWANCE: the two tabletop boards are 72" long and the listed 2x12s are 6 ft, i.e. 72" exactly (packBoards worstSlack 0). You must use both boards at full factory length with no end trimming, or buy longer stock.

CUT LIST APPEARS INCOMPLETE FOR A DESK HEIGHT: the tallest vertical part in the list is the 20" center post, which with a 3/4" cap at each end gives a leg tower about 21 1/2" tall — well short of a normal desk height. Nothing in the plan states the finished height. Confirm your leg height and add the missing parts before cutting.

run1-box-geometry.mjs found no resolvable box for this plan, so the rewrite does not assert which member is captured in the leg tower or how the stacked layers are ordered; those steps describe the technique and the checks without inventing an assembly.

The X-brace parts are given long-point to long-point with 45 degree ends, but the plan does not state the opening they fill; the cross brace's own note says 'cut to fit', and the rewrite treats all the X parts that way.

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body: From 2x4 stock (actual 1 1/2" x 3 1/2") cut two leg tower center posts at 20", four leg tower stretchers at 21", three cross stretchers at 59", eight X-brace diagonals at 10 3/4" and four longer X-brace diagonals at 21 3/4" — all the X pieces measured long point to long point with both ends cut at 45 degrees off square and NOT parallel to each other — plus two cross braces at 30 3/4" with both 45 degree ends parallel. Those parts come to 535 1/2" of 2x4, which needs six 8 ft boards with a 1/8" kerf; the materials list only calls for five, so buy a sixth before you start. From the 8 ft 1x4 cut four leg tower end caps at 18" and four leg tower caps at 3 1/2" — one board covers all eight. Leave the two 2x12s at their full 72" factory length for the tabletop; a 6 ft board is exactly 72", so there is nothing to trim off. Cut the 45 degree pieces last, after the towers are standing, so you can fit them to the opening you actually have.

ops.title: Build the two leg towers
ops.body: Each leg tower is an "I": the 20" 2x4 center post with an 18" 1x4 end cap fastened across each end so the cap projects past the post on both sides. Build both towers at the same time on a flat surface, laying the second one out directly against the first and comparing them part for part — everything downstream bolts to both towers at once, so a difference between them cannot be corrected later. Glue and 2 1/2" self-tapping screws through the caps into the post ends, countersunk so the heads sit flush and disappear under finish. Check that each tower is square across the cap and that the two towers are the same overall height before moving on.

ops.title: Add the bracing
ops.body: Add the 21" leg tower stretchers to each tower, two per tower, keeping them square to the post and identically placed on both towers — measure each stretcher's position from the same end of the post on each tower rather than eyeballing the pair. Predrill every hole with a countersink bit before driving 2 1/2" screws; 2x4 pine splits along the grain if you drive a screw near an end dry, and a split shows through paint. Glue each joint as well as screwing it. Set the two towers side by side again afterward and confirm the stretchers still line up across the pair.

ops.title: Attach the top and bottom caps
ops.body: Fit the four 3 1/2" leg tower caps — one at the top and one at the bottom of each tower — trimming them on the miter saw to sit flush with the surfaces they land on. These are short pieces, so cut them with a stop block or clamp a scrap fence to the saw rather than freehanding four little cuts; a 3 1/2" offcut is also too short to hold safely by hand near the blade. Glue and screw each cap, countersinking as before, and sight down the tower afterward to confirm the caps did not push anything out of line.

ops.title: Stack the 2x4 layers
ops.body: Build up the remaining 2x4 layers on each tower, gluing and screwing each layer to the one below it — the repetition of that stack is the entire design idea, so keep the layers flush at the faces where they show and let any discrepancy fall on the hidden inside face. Work layer for layer on both towers together rather than finishing one tower and then copying it: comparing after every layer catches a 1/16" drift while it is still one screw away from being fixed. Countersink every screw and stagger the screw positions between layers so you are not driving into the last layer's screws. Set the two towers face to face when you are done; they should match everywhere.

ops.title: Join the towers with cross stretchers
ops.body: Stand the two towers upright, parallel and the same way round, and connect them with the three 59" cross stretchers — that length sets the distance between the towers and, with the 72" top, leaves the top overhanging past each tower. Fasten with 2 1/2" pocket screws or countersunk 2 1/2" screws and glue, and clamp everything before driving anything. Measure both diagonals across the assembled base and adjust until they match; from here on the base is what the X brace has to fit into, so a base that is out of square makes every 45 degree cut wrong. Check that all four feet touch the floor at once on a flat surface.

ops.title: Add the X cross bracing
ops.body: Cut the X pieces to the opening you actually have rather than to the list — the two 30 3/4" cross braces carry a 'cut to fit' note and the same applies to the eight 10 3/4" and four 21 3/4" diagonals, whose lengths are long point to long point. Set the miter saw to 45 degrees and cut a test piece from an offcut first, holding it in the opening to confirm the angle points the right way before you commit good stock; on the diagonals the two ends are cut at 45 degrees but NOT parallel to each other, which is easy to get backwards. Work from the center outward so the two arms of the X meet cleanly on the centerline, and dry-fit the whole X before any glue. Fasten with glue and countersunk 2 1/2" screws driven at an angle through the brace ends into the stretchers.

ops.title: Attach the top and finish
ops.body: Join the two 72" 2x12s edge to edge into one top 22 1/2" wide, using 2 1/2" pocket screws and glue along the seam with the boards clamped flat on a level surface — build the top as its own assembly and let the glue cure before it goes anywhere near the base. Alternate the boards' crown so the seam stays flat, and keep the pocket holes on the underside. Then set the top on the base, center it so the overhang is equal at each end, and fasten up through the top stretchers into the underside of the top; a sheet of plywood makes a smoother writing surface if you would rather not deal with a glue seam. Sand every surface up through the grits, ease all four top edges, and apply your finish.

baseStepTitles: Attach the top and bottom caps
Join the towers with cross stretchers

flags: The stringer's angle is the defining cut and the plan does NOT state it — only that the stringers are 13 1/2" with one end cut to the seat/back angle. The rewrite tells you to template and match the two stringers rather than inventing a number.

The back is NOT actually fanned: all three back supports are 8 1/2", so the back is parallel-sided. That length is exactly the 8 1/2" clear width between the two stringers (10" apron less two 3/4" stringers), and five 1 1/2" slats plus four 1/4" gaps comes to exactly 8 1/2" — so the slat spacing is determinate, and the rewrite states it.
Seat-slat gaps are NOT determinate (the stringer's seat run is not stated), so the rewrite gives the technique and calls the gap a builder's choice rather than a number.
Lumber verified with packBoards (1/8" kerf, 96" stock): the 1x3 parts total 55 1/2" and fit one 8 ft board; the 1x2 parts fit the two 8 ft boards listed with room to spare. No shortfall.
run1-box-geometry.mjs found no resolvable box, so no claim is made about which chair member is captured beyond the 8 1/2" back-support arithmetic above.

ops.title: Cut the parts
ops.body: From the 8 ft 1x3 (actual 3/4" x 2 1/2") cut two stringers at 13 1/2", one front apron at 10" and two front legs at 9" — 55 1/2" of parts, so one board covers it all. From the two 8 ft 1x2s (actual 3/4" x 1 1/2") cut four seat slats at 10", five back slats at 14", three back supports at 8 1/2", two arm supports at 9 1/2" and two arms at 9 1/2"; put the five 14" back slats and two seat slats on the first board and everything else on the second. The stringers are the one part that needs real care: one end of each gets cut to the seat and back angle, and that angle is what makes the chair sit like an Adirondack instead of a deck chair. Cut one stringer, then trace it onto the second and cut to the line, or clamp the two together and cut them as a pair, so they are identical even if the angle itself is your own judgement — the plan gives the length but not the angle.

ops.title: Attach the front apron
ops.body: Stand the two stringers on edge, parallel and the same way round, and screw the 10" front apron across their front ends with its ends flush to the outside faces. That 10" is the chair's full outside width and it is what squares the seat frame, so check the frame across both diagonals before the second screw goes in. Predrill every hole — these are 3/4" furring strips and a screw driven dry near an end will split them — then add glue and drive 1 1/4" screws or nails. With the apron on, the clear space between the stringers is 8 1/2", which is exactly the length of the back supports later.

ops.title: Attach the seat slats
ops.body: Lay the four 10" seat slats across the stringers with their ends flush to the outside faces, matching the apron, and space them with an even gap — a stack of coins or a couple of paint stirrers used as a spacer keeps every gap the same, which is the whole visual of an Adirondack seat. Work from the front apron toward the back so any leftover space falls at the rear where it is least visible. Predrill and drive one 1 1/4" screw into each stringer at each slat, with a dab of glue; nails and glue are plenty on a chair that carries a doll. Check that the frame is still square once the last slat is down.

ops.title: Build the back
ops.body: Lay the five 14" back slats face down and set the three 8 1/2" back supports across them. The numbers land exactly: five 1 1/2" slats is 7 1/2", and four 1/4" gaps between them makes 8 1/2" — the same as the supports and the same as the clear space between the stringers, so the whole back drops between them. Use a 1/4" spacer between each pair of slats and keep the slat bottoms aligned on a straight edge, since that bottom line is what sits on the seat. Predrill and fasten each slat to each support with a 1 1/4" screw or nail and glue, putting one support low where the back meets the seat, one at the top and one between.

ops.title: Attach the back and the front legs
ops.body: Set the finished back down between the two stringers, resting on the seat and leaning against the angled rear ends of the stringers — that angled cut is what sets the recline, so let the back follow it rather than forcing it upright. Predrill through the outside face of each stringer into the ends of the back supports and drive 1 1/4" screws with glue. Then attach the two 9" front legs to the outside faces of the stringers at the front, with the apron between them, so they carry the front of the seat and give the arms something to land on. Stand the chair on a flat surface and check that it does not rock before the glue sets.

ops.title: Attach the arm supports
ops.body: Fasten the two 9 1/2" arm supports so each runs from its front leg back to the back assembly, tying the front and rear of the chair into one unit — this is what stops the back from flexing and what holds the arms level. Set both supports to the same height off the seat by measuring each from the seat slats rather than from the floor, and clamp before fastening so nothing shifts as the screw pulls. Predrill, glue and drive 1 1/4" screws at each end. Sight across the two supports from the front; if they are not level with each other, the arms will not be either.

ops.title: Attach the arms and finish
ops.body: Lay the two 9 1/2" arms on top of the arm supports, overhanging the front leg slightly, and fasten each down with glue and two 1 1/4" screws or nails into the support, predrilling first. Sit a doll in it and check both arms rest at the same height before the glue cures. Then sand every surface and round the arm ends, the front edge of the seat and every corner well — a doll chair gets handled far more than it gets sat on, and a splinter on a toy is the one defect that matters. Finish it if you like, or leave it bare.

flags: The 20" legs are never cut anywhere in the source — the only breakdown step covers plywood. The rewrite adds them to that step (four legs at 20" from the 8 ft 2x2 uses 80" of 96").
The 1x6 and 1x3 substitutes are sufficient: four box sides at 15 3/4" is 63" of a 72" 6 ft 1x6, and four aprons at 15 3/4" is 63" of the 6 ft 1x3. Verified with a 1/8" kerf.
'Half sheet' is not defined in the plan, and the existing step tells you to rip strips 'the length of the sheet', so the rewrite gives part sizes and strip widths without asserting a sheet size.

run1-box-geometry.mjs found no resolvable box; the 15 3/4" bottom fitting the 15 3/4" opening between legs follows from the plan's own instruction to attach the sides flush to the insides of the legs, and is stated as a check rather than as an assembly claim.
Overall table height (20" legs plus the 3/4" top) and the 28" petal spread (a 14" radius, doubled) are arithmetic on the plan's own numbers.

ops.title: Break down the stock
ops.body: This can be built from plywood scraps for the storage box, or from the 1x6 and 1x3 boards as a substitute. When cutting the plywood, make long straight full-length cuts to conserve material: rip a strip for the 5 1/2"-wide box sides and a strip for the 2 1/2"-wide aprons down the length of the sheet, then cross-cut both strips into four pieces each at 15 3/4", and cut the 31 1/2" square tabletop blank and the 15 3/4" square box bottom from what is left. If you are using boards instead, the four 15 3/4" sides use 63" of the 6 ft 1x6 and the four 15 3/4" aprons use 63" of the 6 ft 1x3. Cut four legs at 20" from the 8 ft 2x2, which uses 80" of the board. Measure and cut as you go rather than marking every cut up front, since the blade's kerf eats into each mark.

ops.title: Lay out the flower shape
ops.body: Find the center of the 31 1/2" square blank by drawing both diagonals, then mark a point 14" out from that center along several reference lines radiating from it — a pencil tied to a string pinned at the center draws those points accurately. At a 14" radius the petal tips reach 28" across the 31 1/2" blank, which leaves about 1 3/4" of waste all around, so the layout has to be centered or you will run off the edge. Draw an arch between each neighboring pair of points to make the petals, and make a paper or cardboard template of one arch that you trace, flip and repeat around the circle — free-handing each petal separately almost never comes out even, and unevenness in a shape this simple is the only thing anyone will notice.

ops.title: Cut the flower shape
ops.body: Support the blank on sawhorses or scrap so the whole outline is clear underneath and nothing pinches the blade, then cut the petals out with the jigsaw, following just outside your line and letting the blade do the work through the turns. Cut the outside curve of each petal in one pass where you can; stopping and restarting mid-curve leaves a flat spot that is obvious once the top is finished. Use a fine-tooth or down-cut blade to hold the plywood's top veneer together at the cut. Sand the sawn edge fair afterward, checking the profile by eye from a few feet back, and ease the edge well — this is a kid's table and that edge is exactly at head height for a toddler.

ops.title: Build the storage box base
ops.body: Drill two 3/4"-setting pocket holes at each end of each 15 3/4" box side and attach them to the 20" legs with 1 1/4" pocket screws, flush to the insides of the legs so the leg faces stay proud. Build two leg-and-side subassemblies first — two legs joined by one side each — and only then join the remaining two sides to close the box; joining four sides to four legs all at once is where this goes out of square. Hold the sides up from the bottom of the legs so there is a 2" gap underneath for floor clearance, and use a scrap block cut to that height as a gauge under every leg rather than measuring four times. Check both diagonals across the closed box before the last screws go in.

ops.title: Attach the storage box bottom
ops.body: The 15 3/4" square plywood bottom is the same length as the sides, so it should drop into the opening between the legs with the sides' inside faces around it — if it will not go in, the box is out of square and now is when you fix that, not after the top is on. Slide it down flush with the bottom edges of the sides and fasten up through the sides into its edges, or set it on cleats if you would rather not see fasteners in the side faces. It doubles as the shelf everything sits on, so glue it as well as screwing it.

ops.title: Add the aprons
ops.body: The four 15 3/4" aprons sit on top of the base in the same footprint as the sides, tying the leg tops together and giving the tabletop something to land on across its whole width. Attach each one flush to the insides of the leg tops with 1 1/4" pocket screws, working in the same two-subassemblies-then-close order as the box. Before fastening, drill one extra pocket hole in the center of each apron facing UPWARD — that is what the tabletop screws into in the next step, and it is far easier to drill now on the bench than overhead later.

ops.title: Attach the tabletop
ops.body: Turn the flower top face down and set the base on it upside down, lining the outside corners of the legs up with the inside points between petals and shifting the base until every petal tip is the same distance from the leg corners — measure two opposite tips, then the other two, rather than trusting your eye. Mark the base position, then drive 1 1/4" screws up through the pocket holes in the aprons into the underside of the top. Turn it right side up: the legs are 20" and the top is 3/4", so the finished table stands about 20 3/4" tall. Sand every surface and edge smooth, round the petal edges thoroughly, and finish.

flags: MATERIAL MIS-TAG in the source: step 4 tags the oven door to 'Pine, 1x3, 2 ft', but the door frame parts are 3 1/2" wide in the cut list, i.e. 1x4. The 1x3 2 ft piece is the 22" x 2 1/2" front bottom rail. Both tags corrected in the rewrite.

The door geometry is fully determinate and is now stated: two 14 1/2" sides plus two 15" top/bottom pieces, all 3 1/2" wide, make a door 22" wide x 14 1/2" tall with a 15" x 7 1/2" window opening — 22" is exactly the box's outside width (20 1/2" bottom between two 3/4" sides), and the 8" x 16" plexiglass covers that opening with about 1/2" of overlap all around.

Front elevation checks out: 3 1/2" top rail + 14 1/2" door + 2 1/2" bottom rail = 20 1/2" against a 20 3/4" box front, leaving about 1/4" of total clearance for the door. Tight but workable — trim the door edge if your hinges need more.

Lumber verified with packBoards (1/8" kerf, 96" stock): the four 1x12 carcass parts total 82 1/2" and fit one 8 ft board; the front top rail and all four door frame parts total 81" and fit the one 8 ft 1x4. No shortfall.

run1-box-geometry.mjs found no resolvable box, but the cut list proves the carcass on its own: 20 1/2" bottom + 3/4" + 3/4" = the 22" top, and the 22" x 20 3/4" back equals the outside front elevation (20" sides + the 3/4" top). The rewrite follows that reading.

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body: From the 8 ft 1x12 (actual 3/4" x 11 1/4") cut the top at 22", two sides at 20" and the bottom at 20 1/2" — 82 1/2" of parts, so one board covers the carcass. From the 8 ft 1x4 cut the front top rail at 22", two door frame sides at 14 1/2" and two door frame top/bottom pieces at 15"; that is 81" and again fits the one board. Cut the front bottom rail 22" long from the 2 ft 1x3, and the stovetop grate strip 18 1/2" long from the 1x2. Cut the 1/4" plywood back 22" x 20 3/4", which is the exact outside face of the finished box and will be your squaring check later. Square every cut and label the pieces; the 20" sides and the 20 1/2" bottom are close enough in length to mix up on a bench.

ops.title: Build the basic box
ops.body: The two 20" sides stand vertically and the 20 1/2" bottom fits between them, which makes the carcass 22" wide outside — exactly the length of the top, which then lands across both sides. Assemble with 3/4" pocket holes and 1 1/4" pocket screws, or 1 1/4" brads and glue, and put glue in every joint: the source is explicit that every joint on this build gets reinforced, and a toy stove takes more abuse per square inch than anything else in the house. Check both diagonals across the box before the glue sets, and recheck after each of the next two steps — a rack in the carcass shows up immediately as an oven door that will not close evenly, and it is unfixable once the back is on.

ops.title: Attach the back
ops.body: The 22" x 20 3/4" plywood back matches the box's outside face exactly, so line one corner up, nail that edge, then pull the opposite corner into line with the panel's edge before nailing the rest — the panel squares the carcass permanently. Use the shorter 3/4" screws or brads here; a 1 1/4" fastener driven into the 3/4" edge of a side tends to split out and buys you nothing on a 1/4" back. Nail into the sides, the top and the bottom every few inches, with glue behind it. Check the diagonals one last time before the last edge goes down.

ops.title: Attach the front pieces
ops.body: The two front rails are both 22", the full outside width of the box. The 3 1/2"-wide 1x4 goes on at the top of the front opening and the 2 1/2"-wide 1x3 at the bottom, and between them they leave about 14 3/4" of clear front opening for the oven door. Set both with their ends flush to the outside faces of the sides, and fasten with 1 1/4" brads and glue into the side edges and into the top and bottom boards. Check for square again before the glue grabs, and measure the actual opening you have ended up with — the door is built to that number, not to the list.

ops.title: Build and hang the oven door
ops.body: The door is a frame of 3 1/2"-wide 1x4: two 14 1/2" sides with the two 15" top and bottom pieces between them, which makes a full-overlay door 22" wide by 14 1/2" tall — the same 22" as the box — with a 15" x 7 1/2" window opening in the middle. Build it flat with 3/4" pocket holes and 1 1/4" pocket screws plus glue, checking the diagonals, since a racked door will never sit right against a square box. Lay the 8" x 16" plexiglass over the opening from behind; it overlaps about 1/2" all around, so drill a clearance hole (slightly oversize, so the plastic can move without cracking) in each corner and drive one 3/4" wood screw per corner — four screws is all it needs. Hang the door on the surface-mount self-closing hinges, and expect roughly 1/4" of total clearance between the two front rails, so trim the door's top or bottom edge slightly if the hinges need more.

ops.title: Add feet (optional)
ops.body: Feet lift the stove off the floor and give it an appliance-like stance, and they also keep the bottom board off a damp floor. Cut four short blocks from the 1x2 and fasten them up into the bottom board near each corner with glue and screws, setting them in far enough from the edges that a kid pushing on the top corner cannot tip the stove over its own foot. Make all four the same length — cut them with a stop block — and check the finished stove for rock on a flat floor before anything else goes on.

ops.title: Build the stovetop
ops.body: Cut the 18 1/2" grate strip into short pieces and lay them out on the 22" x 11 1/4" top in two burner-sized groups, spacing them so each group reads as a grate; move them around dry until the layout looks right before any glue goes down. Nail them with 1 1/4" brads and glue, driving into the top board and keeping the nails away from the ends of the short pieces so they do not split. Then set the four burner knobs: glue a 1" dowel piece to the back of each 2" wood round, mark the four knob positions across the front top rail evenly and on one level line, and fasten each dowel into the rail. Sand every edge and corner of the finished stove well — this is a toy, so nothing should be sharp anywhere a child can reach.

baseStepTitles: Build and hang the oven door

flags: DIMENSION DEFECT: the cubby dividers are listed at 12 3/4", but the clear interior of a cubby is 13 1/2" (a 15" side less the 3/4" top and 3/4" bottom that fit between the sides). The dividers are 3/4" short. The rewrite says to cut them to the opening you measure.

The assembly IS determinate from the cut list even though run1-box-geometry.mjs reported no box: the 15" sides run the full height with the 54" and 62" top/bottoms between them, giving outside lengths of 55 1/2" and 63 1/2" — exactly the listed end trim (55 1/2") and side trim (63 1/2") — and the 12" vertical trim then fits exactly between 1 1/2" top and bottom trim on a 15" tall box (15 - 1 1/2 - 1 1/2 = 12). Every prose number follows that reading.

Platform geometry, all arithmetic on the plan's own parts: the 55 1/2" end cubby less two 11 1/4" side cubby depths leaves a 33" channel; the 2 1/2"-wide cleats project into it from each side, so the 30 1/2" slats land about 1 1/4" onto each cleat. Finished frame is about 55 1/2" x 74 3/4" — a full mattress is 54" x 75", so the platform is about 1/4" shorter than the mattress and the mattress will overhang the end cubby very slightly.

Lumber verified with packBoards (1/8" kerf, 96" stock): the 1x12 parts need exactly 6 boards, the 1x3 parts exactly 5 — both matching the materials list. The 1x2 parts fit within the 6 boards listed. No shortfall anywhere.

step.title: Cut all the parts
step.body: From the six 8 ft 1x12s (actual 3/4" x 11 1/4") cut six cubby sides at 15", three cubby dividers at 12 3/4", two end cubby top/bottoms at 54" and four side cubby top/bottoms at 62". Those parts need all six boards: put a 62" piece with two 15" sides on each of two boards, a 62" piece with one 15" side and one divider on each of two more, and the two 54" pieces on the last two along with the remaining divider. From the six 8 ft 1x2s cut six vertical trims at 12", two end top/bottom trims at 55 1/2" and four side top/bottom trims at 63 1/2". From the five 8 ft 1x3s cut two center support cleats at 63 1/2" and eleven center support slats at 30 1/2" — that is exactly five boards, with a cleat and a slat on each of the first two and three slats on each of the last three. Have the lumber yard rip the 1/4" plywood sheet into 15" strips before you leave, then cut the three cubby backs to length once each box is built. Square every cut and label the pieces.
materials: 1/4" plywood, ripped to 15 in strips, full sheet

ops.title: Build the end cubby
ops.body: The two 15" sides run the full height of the box and the 54" top and bottom fit between them, which makes the end cubby 55 1/2" long outside — the same 55 1/2" as the end trim that goes on later, which is how you know the assembly is right. Fasten through the outside faces of the sides into the ends of the top and bottom with 2" screws and glue. Then fit the divider midway along the box: it is listed at 12 3/4", but the actual opening between the top and bottom works out to 13 1/2" (15" less two 3/4" boards), so measure your own opening and cut the divider to it rather than to the list, or you will end up with a loose partition. Check both diagonals before the glue sets.

ops.title: Build the two side cubbies
ops.body: Build both side cubbies exactly the same way, with the 15" sides running full height and the 62" top and bottom between them, giving 63 1/2" outside — matching the 63 1/2" side trim. Build them one after the other on the same flat surface and compare them before moving on; these two boxes carry the slat platform between them, so a difference in height between them becomes a bed that rocks. Fit each divider to the opening you measure, same as on the end cubby. Check the diagonals on both boxes.

ops.title: Attach the backs
ops.body: The 15" plywood strips match the 15" height of every box, so all you need is one crosscut per back: 55 1/2" for the end cubby and 63 1/2" for each side cubby. Cut each one to the box you are about to put it on rather than to the list, then set it on, line up one corner, nail that edge with 1 1/4" finish nails and glue, and pull the opposite corner into line before nailing the rest. Nail into the center divider as well as the outer sides — the divider is what stops the middle of a long back from drumming and what makes the whole box stop racking. A back nailed on square is what locks each cubby permanently square.
materials: 1/4" plywood, ripped to 15 in strips, full sheet

ops.title: Add the trim
ops.body: The 1x2 trim covers the plywood edges and the screw heads on the front faces. Run the top and bottom trim first — 55 1/2" pieces on the end cubby and 63 1/2" pieces on the side cubbies, which is the full outside length of each box — then drop the six 12" vertical trims in between them. Those numbers close exactly: a 15" tall box less 1 1/2" of trim at the top and 1 1/2" at the bottom leaves 12", so a vertical trim that does not fit means the horizontal trim is not sitting flush. Put one vertical trim over each box's divider and one at each end of the side cubbies. Glue and 2" finish nails throughout.

ops.title: Attach the cleats
ops.body: The two 63 1/2" cleats run the full length of the side cubbies and go on the BACK face of each one — the face that will look inward toward the middle of the bed — with the end cubby getting no cleats at all. Fasten each with 2" screws and glue, driving into the sides, the divider and the top and bottom boards, not just into the plywood back. Set both cleats at the same height, measured from the top of each box rather than from the floor, and check them against each other with a straightedge across the two boxes: the slat platform rests on these two strips, so any difference between them is a permanent tilt in the mattress. The 2 1/2" cleat projects into the opening, which is what gives the slats something to land on.

ops.title: Lay the slats
ops.body: Stand the two side cubbies parallel with the end cubby across one end; the 55 1/2" end cubby less the two 11 1/4" side cubby depths leaves a 33" channel between them, and with a 2 1/2" cleat projecting from each side the 30 1/2" slats land about 1 1/4" onto each cleat. Lay all eleven slats across, spaced evenly along the length, and fasten each end down with 1 1/4" screws and NO glue — that is deliberate, and it is what lets the whole bed come apart for a move instead of having to be broken. Screw the end cubby to the ends of the slats as well, so the three boxes and the platform become one frame. Check that every slat has full bearing on both cleats before you screw it.

ops.title: Assemble the bed
ops.body: Bring the three boxes together in the room where the bed will live rather than assembling elsewhere and trying to carry it — the finished frame is about 55 1/2" wide and 74 3/4" long and will not go around a corner. Screw the side cubbies to the end cubby with 2" screws through the meeting faces, checking that the tops of all three boxes sit in one plane so the mattress does not sit on a step. The mattress lands on the slat platform in the middle and on the tops of the two side cubbies at the edges, so the platform surface and the cubby tops need to be flush. A full mattress is 54" x 75", so it will fit the width with room to spare and overhang the end by about a quarter inch.

ops.title: Finish
ops.body: Fill every screw and nail hole with wood filler, let it dry hard, and sand it back flush — on a piece with this much visible trim, filled holes are most of what separates it from shop furniture. Sand all the outside faces and round over the top edges of the cubbies, which are exactly where hands and shins land when someone sits on the bed. Prime and paint, or stain and seal with poly, and get the inside faces of the cubbies too, since they are open storage and will be looked into. Let the finish cure fully before the mattress goes on.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

flags: RIBBON QUANTITY IS THIN, and the plan does not state how many ribbons per joint: 12 blocks make 11 joints, and the 2 yards (72") listed works out to about 6 1/2" of ribbon per joint — enough for ONE ribbon per joint, not for the traditional three-ribbons-per-joint version, which would need roughly three times that. The rewrite states the arithmetic and tells the builder to buy more ribbon if going the traditional route rather than inventing a piece length.
The plan gives no ribbon piece length, so the rewrite says to cut a test piece against two real blocks and use it as the pattern rather than quoting a number.
Brad length is not specified. A block is 3/4" thick, so the rewrite says to use brads short enough not to break through the opposite face — the one safety-relevant consequence on a toy.
This plan is published: false in the source.
run1-box-geometry.mjs found no resolvable box, which is expected for a single-part cut list.

ops.title: Cut the blocks
ops.body: Cut twelve blocks 3" long from the 2 1/2"-wide hobby board, which is 3/4" thick — 36" of board plus saw kerfs, well inside the material listed. Clamp a stop block to the miter saw fence and cut all twelve against it without moving it, so every block is identical: the illusion depends on the blocks folding flush against each other at every joint, and even a 1/32" variation from block to block breaks the cascade. Do not measure and mark twelve times; that is exactly how the variation creeps in. Check the last block against the first when you are done, and cut a thirteenth as a spare if the board allows.

ops.title: Round the corners and sand
ops.body: Round over all four corners and every edge of each block, and sand the faces smooth — this is a toy that gets flipped hand to hand for hours, so a sharp arris is the defect that actually matters. Keep the rounding light and consistent from block to block; taking too much off an end shortens the flat that the next block has to lie against and softens the snap of the fold. Sand the faces flat rather than rounding them, and blow or wipe the dust off before paint.

ops.title: Paint the blocks
ops.body: Paint each block a different color on each face and edge — the shifting colors as the blocks tumble are most of what makes people keep flipping it. Work in thin coats and let each dry fully; a heavy build of paint on the faces adds thickness that keeps the blocks from folding flush, and that is the one thing that can kill the illusion after the blocks were cut perfectly. Paint the faces where the ribbons will land too, so nothing shows bare once it is assembled, and let everything cure hard before the ribbons go on.

ops.title: Cut the ribbons
ops.body: Twelve blocks make eleven joints, and each joint needs ribbon long enough to run across the face of one block, wrap over the end and land on the face of the next with a little to fasten at each end. Cut one test piece against two real blocks, fold it exactly as it will sit, and only once that piece works use it as the pattern for the rest — cutting all the ribbon first to a guessed length is how you end up 1/2" short eleven times. The 2 yards listed is 72", which is about 6 1/2" per joint and enough for a single ribbon at each joint; the traditional version uses three narrow ribbons per joint and needs roughly three times that much, so buy more if that is the one you want. Seal each cut end with a quick pass of flame or a dab of clear glue so the grosgrain does not fray.

ops.title: Attach the ribbons
ops.body: Lay two blocks end to end and fasten the ribbon so it runs down the back of the first block, over the gap and onto the back of the second, then add the next block with its ribbon attached on the OPPOSITE face from the one before — that alternation down the whole stack is the entire mechanism; get it right for two joints and just repeat it nine more times. Work one joint at a time down the row and test the fold as you go rather than nailing all eleven and hoping, because a joint fastened on the wrong face stops the cascade dead at that point. Fasten with small brads short enough not to break through the 3/4" block on the far side, and keep the ribbon flat and snug, with just enough slack for the blocks to close on each other. Hold the top block and let the stack tumble to test; if it stops partway, that joint's ribbon is on the wrong face or is too tight.

flags: GARBLED SENTENCE in the source, step 3: "Put the front pocket holes INSIDE the box (the drawer face frame hides interior faces later is not true — the outside face shows through the opening)." It contradicts itself mid-parenthesis. Rewritten as an intelligible instruction.

OPENING CONTRADICTION (real, flagged not fixed): the 70 1/2" frame rails with four 3 1/2" uprights give three bays of about 18 13/16", but the listed drawer parts make a box 20 1/2" wide outside, and the face frame (72" rails, four 1 1/2" stiles) implies 22" openings. These three numbers cannot all be right. The cut list already says CUT TO FIT for every drawer part; the rewrite makes that instruction load-bearing — build the boxes and the face frame to the bays you actually measure.

The frame geometry IS determinate: two 3 1/2"-wide rails laid flat (1 1/2" thick) with 14 1/4" uprights between them makes a frame 17 1/4" tall, which is exactly the side panel height, and 70 1/2" plus two 3/4" side panels is exactly the 72" top. Bench height is 17 1/4" + 3/4" = 18", and the face frame (1 1/2" + 13" + 3 1/2") also sums to 18".
run1-box-geometry.mjs: on the drawer box, the 23 7/8" sides run the FULL depth and the 19" fronts/backs sit BETWEEN them, proved by the 20 1/2" x 23 7/8" bottoms. The rewrite follows that reading.

TIGHT STOCK, verified with packBoards (1/8" kerf, 96"): the drawer parts need all three 1x10s with only 1/8" of slack on the worst board — and those parts are the ones marked cut-to-fit, so buy a fourth board if your slides call for different sizes. The 1x4 face frame bottom rail is 72" from a 6 ft (72") board, i.e. zero trim allowance. The five 2x4s cover the frames with about 38" left over for the slide spacers.

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body: Rip the 3/4" plywood sheet into two 96" x 23 7/8" strips with a straightedge guide clamped to the sheet — freehanding a 96" rip is how the top ends up out of parallel. From one strip cut the top at 72" long; from the other cut two side panels 23 7/8" x 17 1/4". From the five 8 ft 2x4s cut four frame rails at 70 1/2" and eight uprights at 14 1/4" — one rail plus one upright fits on each of four boards and the last four uprights fit the fifth, which leaves roughly 38" of that board for the drawer slide spacers later. From the 1x2s cut the face frame top rail at 72" and four stiles at 13"; the face frame bottom rail is the full 72" length of the 6 ft 1x4, so use it at its factory length with nothing trimmed off. Cut the 1x10 drawer parts and the 1/4" drawer bottoms LAST, after the carcass is built and the slides are in hand — every drawer row in the cut list is marked cut to fit, and the listed sizes use all three 1x10s with almost no slack, so buy a fourth board if your slides call for different numbers. Label everything as it comes off the saw.

ops.title: Build two IDENTICAL 2x4 frames
ops.body: Each frame is a ladder: two 70 1/2" rails laid flat with four 14 1/4" uprights standing between them, which makes a frame 17 1/4" tall — the same height as the plywood side panels, and 3/4" of top later brings the bench to 18". Fasten with 3" self-tapping screws, or 1 1/2"-setting pocket holes and 2 1/2" pocket screws, plus glue at every joint. Build the second frame stacked directly on top of the first, corner to corner and upright over upright, and clamp them together while you fasten: the drawer slides bridge from the front frame to the back frame, so 'identical' here is not a virtue, it is the difference between drawers that glide and drawers that bind forever. Measure both diagonals on each frame and against each other before unstacking them.

ops.title: Skin the ends
ops.body: Stand the two frames parallel and cap each end with a 23 7/8" x 17 1/4" plywood side panel, which sets the bench depth and locks the two frames at a fixed spacing. The panel is 17 1/4" tall, exactly the frame height, and 70 1/2" of rail plus a 3/4" panel at each end comes to 72" — the length of the top, which is your check that the carcass came out right. Fasten with 2" screws, or brads and glue, into the ends of both rails and the end uprights. Check that the assembly is square and that both frames are still parallel before the glue sets, because from here on every drawer opening is measured out of this box.

ops.title: Build the drawer boxes — to your slides, not the list
ops.body: Read your slides' instructions first and measure the three actual bays in the carcass, because the plan's own numbers do not agree with each other here: the 70 1/2" rails with four 3 1/2" uprights give bays of about 18 13/16", the listed drawer parts make a box 20 1/2" wide, and the face frame implies 22" openings. Your measured bay is the only number that counts. For standard 1/2"-clearance side-mount slides, make each box the bay width minus 1", and make the sides as long as the slides. Build each box with the two sides running the full depth and the front and back fitting BETWEEN them, so the box's outside width is the front/back length plus 3/4" at each side, and the bottom is that same outside width by the side length. If you pocket-hole the boxes, put the pocket holes on the INSIDE faces — the outside faces of the box show through the opening every time the drawer is pulled, and nothing later covers them. Bottoms go on with glue and 3/4" brads or narrow-crown staples, and a bottom nailed on square is what keeps the box square for good.

ops.title: Mount the cabinet slide members
ops.body: Cut 2x4 scraps to the height you want each slide to sit and stand them inside the bench as spacers, then rest each cabinet-side slide member on its spacer and screw it to the frame uprights. Using a cut spacer instead of a pencil mark is what guarantees the left and right members of a pair land at exactly the same height, and matched heights are the entire game with drawers. Do all six members off the same pair of spacers, and check each pair with a level along its length before driving the second screw. Work front frame and back frame together for each slide, since the slide has to bridge from one to the other.

ops.title: Mount the drawer members and hang the boxes
ops.body: Attach the drawer-side slide members to the boxes exactly where the slide instructions specify, measuring their setback from the front edge of the box the same on both sides of every drawer. Then lift each box into its cabinet members until the slides click home. Fit the boxes one at a time and try each one before hanging the next, so a problem is traceable to one drawer rather than to three.

ops.title: Build and attach the face frame
ops.body: Measure the assembled front before you cut anything to final length: the frame gets a 72" top rail, a 72" bottom rail and four 13" stiles, which sums to 18" tall and matches the bench exactly, but the stile POSITIONS have to land over the frame uprights, not on the listed spacing — the plan's face frame implies wider openings than the carcass actually has. Pocket-hole the whole frame together flat on a bench with a 3/4" setting and 1 1/4" screws, checking the diagonals; building it as one unit and then hanging it is why the reveals come out even, and it is much harder to fix piece by piece on the front of the bench. Then glue the completed frame to the carcass and tack it with brads, clamping across the face while the glue sets.

ops.title: Face the drawers
ops.body: Size each drawer face frame to the opening it covers rather than to the listed lengths, which the cut list itself marks approximate — measure each of the three openings separately, since they will not be identical. Build the three 1x3 frames flat and check them for square, then hang each one on its box holding an even 1/8" gap all the way around; stacks of playing cards make good shims, and setting the bottom shims first lets the face sit on them while you fix it. Tack with brads and glue, then pull the drawer and add a screw from inside the box into the back of the face so it cannot work loose. Add the knobs, sand and finish the whole piece, easing the front edge of the top well — the backs of knees meet it every time someone ties a shoe.

baseStepTitles: Build the drawer boxes — to your slides, not the list
Mount the drawer members and hang the boxes
Build and attach the face frame
